# Mirodan PhD Vol 2 — Chapter I: Basic Concepts (extract)

Source: /Users/opaque/Downloads/Mirodan-PhD-1997-Vol2.pdf, printed pp. 263-364
Extractor: parallel chapter agent
Status: complete (extracted to end of Chapter I, p. 364)

**Page mapping verified**: PDF page = printed page − 261. Chapter I covers PDF pp. 2–103 (printed 263–364). Vol 2 is "The System" — a glossary-driven exposition of the Laban/Carpenter/Malmgren vocabulary. NB from p.262: "the system actually consists of a specialist vocabulary. Laban and Malmgren have taken some seventy English words to which they gave new or altered interpretations." Capitalised terms = system-specific usage.

## 1. Effort and Action — definitions

Mirodan opens Chapter I quoting Carpenter's "Glossary" verbatim (p. 264):

> "EFFORT — the sequence of Inner Attitudes and Externalized Drives which activates (initiates) an action."

> "ACTION (outer action) — a bodily movement (expression) expressed through the Motion Factors of weight, space, time and flow, performed for a functional purpose with a measure of conscious volition."

Note Malmgren added the words "outer action" to Carpenter's original Action definition (footnote, p. 273). Effort is a *sequence/process*, not a thing. Effort includes BOTH inner and outer; it is "the sum (the 'sequence' says the definition) of the psychologically generated energies which move ('activate') the body into action — the battery which moves the hand of the clock" (p. 275).

"Functional purpose" is critical (p. 271): "All these movements are Actions (with a capital A) precisely because (and only when) they have a purpose." Movement without a purpose is not Action. This is where the system breaks with Method — Malmgren attacks the Method actor who crosses to pour a drink with no functional connection to intention. For Stanislavski/Malmgren the move must be functional in two senses: it achieves something concrete AND it expresses the relationship/intention (p. 272).

Distance from naturalism (footnote p. 272): "the system distances itself from naturalism… and draws on the expressionist school which considered all artistic movement to be imbued with meaning."

## 2. Inner / Outer dichotomy

Mirodan deliberately complicates the simple Inner/Outer split. Stanislavski's "the division between them is vague" is quoted (p. 265). The actor "aims to achieve an uninterrupted flow of psychological energy between the inner and the outer."

Crucially Malmgren goes further than a simple binary: each side has its OWN inner/outer split. Four layers total (p. 273):

- **Inner Character** = "character essence" / Psychological type (Jung)
- **Outer Character** = professional or other 'persona' (the Mask)
- **Inner Action** = character thought-process (Stanislavskian sub-text)
- **Outer Action** = physical and psychological activities ("doings") directed towards other characters — the only one visible to the audience

The "hour-glass / figure-8 / tree of energy" (p. 273): performer at the centre of an hour-glass. Two spheres of energy, joined at the actor's centre. Reduce to a flat figure 8 — two mirroring rings:
- **Extravert energy**: away from actor toward the outer; intuitively felt as "forward" in space, "future" in time, "upwards" in weight (against gravity).
- **Introvert energy**: inwards toward innermost Character; "backwards" in space, "past" in time, "downwards" in weight (giving in to gravitational pull).

Then a "final leap": conceive the spheres NOT outside the performer but INSIDE him (p. 274) — body now encompasses the spheres, internal circulation = thoughts/sensations/emotions = the character's organic self.

## 3. Character vs. Action

The crucial distinction (p. 265):

> "On the one side we have the character. The character as he is outside relationships, in his room, in his bed, with the blankets pulled over his head. The character defined by psychological characteristics of which the actor is conscious, but which the character himself perceives only dimly, if at all… On the other side we have the Action: those things which the character does and through which he is revealed, those things of which he is fully conscious."

Within Character itself, Malmgren imports Pirandello's Face/Mask split (p. 268):
- **Face** = unchangeable self, innermost psychological characteristics. Character is NOT conscious of his Face.
- **Mask** = the persona worn for social pressures, "given circumstances", relationships. Character is "semi-conscious" of his Mask, may glimpse it in moments of introspection.

Mask is built from "professional deformation" (p. 269) — soldiers drilled, policeman walking the beat. Plus relational role (father vs lover) and national/social type. The Mask is recognisable (a "type"), the Face is unique. "But no mask is truly interesting without a face behind it. The Face gives it life" (p. 270).

The 4-layer hour-glass (p. 274):
> "Yat Malmgren therefore considers that Character and Action have two sides each, like the two sides of a spoon: a 'concave' Inner and a 'convex' Outer."

## 4. Motion Factors (Weight, Space, Time, Flow)

Mapping is established explicitly (p. 266) following Jung:

> a. Sensing 'presses out' the sensuous energy of **Weight**, creating movement with or against gravity.
> b. Thinking 'ex-presses' itself as mental energy 'travelling' through **Space**. Thought 'moves', creating bodily movement in space.
> c. Intuition 'ex-presses' temporal relationships, creating connections between past and future, with the movement occurring in **Time**.
> d. Feeling 'presses out' emotional energy, which pervades all other forms of movement and is perceived as co-ordination or **Flow**.

So Mental Function → Motion Factor is a strict 1:1 correspondence: Sensing↔Weight, Thinking↔Space, Intuiting↔Time, Feeling↔Flow.

Etymological note (footnote p. 266): Laban's German *ausdruck* = expression = literally a "pressing out". Hence Mirodan's repeated 'press out' framing.

## 5. Mental Functions and their mapping

The Mental Factors are Carpenter's name for Jung's four psychological functions, given gerundive form to emphasise dynamic/energy-generating role (p. 277):

> sensing / thinking / intuiting / feeling — "the four psychological concepts" ("Book" p. 2)

Mental Factors are LATENT and PASSIVE (p. 279): "Mental Factors do not act upon the reality around us. They are latent and are only affected by reality."

The 'television screens' image (p. 279): four blank screens at birth. Reality registers experience onto each; the actor systematically increases sensitivity of each function. "At their peak, accomplished actors keep all four Mental Factors 'alive' at the same time. They are then recognized as having 'presence' and making an 'impact' on the audience. Such an actor is *long before he does anything*." Marlene Dietrich, Olivier — the cited examples of "endless volume".

**Inner Participations** are the active counterparts (p. 280):
> "intending / attending / deciding / adapting — revealed in our movements by the Motion Factors of weight, space, time, and flow respectively." ("Book" p. 5)

So the Mental Factor (latent) → Inner Participation (active) → Motion Factor (visible) chain runs:
- Sensing → Intending → Weight
- Thinking → Attending → Space
- Intuiting → Deciding → Time
- Feeling → Adapting → Flow

Definitions of the Inner Participations (p. 281, Mirodan's summary of Malmgren's discourse):
- **Intending**: active Sensing — intentions, desires, needs, the 'will-power' to act on given circumstances.
- **Attending**: active Thinking — the cerebral function of directed, focused attention used to understand and solve problems.
- **Deciding**: active Intuiting — the capacity to take subliminal (unconscious) decisions based on past experience.
- **Adapting**: active Feeling — the capacity to react/adjust emotionally to given circumstances.

Critical: Mental Factor → Inner Participation transition requires an OUTSIDE catalyst. Mental Factors are self-activated (latent); Inner Participations are by-product of catalysed engagement with the objective world (p. 282).

**Order matters and is contested** (footnotes pp. 277, 281): Laban started with Attending (Objective perceived) → Intending (strength summoned) → Deciding (decision to act) → Adapting (flow at moment of action). Malmgren's "Book" follows Jung's "academic" order — Sensation/Thinking/Intuition/Feeling. But Malmgren *prefers in practice* an "organic" order: Feeling first (a free emotional state / inner relaxation is prerequisite for Sensing), then Sensing, then Intuiting and Thinking. "This, as Stanislavski has shown… is not the relaxation of the body on the beach, but an emotionally 'neutral', receptive state akin to that achieved by masters of martial arts." (p. 267)

### Body 'spheres' (Russian doll, p. 285-286)

Malmgren divides the body into four concentric spheres centred on regions of the spine — analogous to chakras / Grotowski's "sources of energy":

1. **Lumbar sphere** (5 lumbar vertebrae, abdomen + lower back) — NOT directly expressive; provides impulse for movement in all 3 directions. Associated with **Feeling**.
2. **Dorsal sphere** (12 dorsal vertebrae, trunk hip-to-shoulder-blades) — activated by **Adapting**. Emotional states generate trunk movement.
3. **Cervical sphere** (5 lower cervical vertebrae) — neck, arms, legs MINUS extremities. Moved by **Intending**. Sensuous reactions.
4. **Atlas/Axis sphere** (2 upper cervical vertebrae) — head, hands, feet. Moved by **Attending**. Cerebral/intellectual energy.

So: extremities = thought, limbs = sensing, trunk = emotion, core = relaxation/feeling-source.

### Tempo per Inner Participation (p. 286)

> "Inner Participations as a whole are revealed physically in the overall tempo of the character's movement. Tempo itself, being a function of Time, is the physical realization of the Inner Participation of Deciding. Subconscious Deciding is the basis upon which all other Inner Participations express themselves 'in time', reflecting the role of *primum mobile* played by Deciding in the order of the Inner Participations."

- **Attending**: fastest tempo — thought travels at the speed of light.
- **Intending**: medium tempo — muscular exertion against gravity.
- **Adapting**: slowest tempo — emotion swings/spirals like circulation of blood.

(Note: this is the first appearance of Physical Tempo as a concept — and it is anchored to Deciding/Time as *primum mobile*.)

### Towards / Away From — energy direction (pp. 282-284)

Movement of energy between Inner Participation and Objective always either toward or away from the Objective, in continuous flow on the figure-8. Each Inner Participation produces specific energy:
- **Intending** = sensuous attracting (toward) / repelling (away)
- **Attending** = cerebral approving (toward) / denying (away)
- **Adapting** = emotional liking (toward) / disliking (away)
- **Deciding** = THE EXCEPTION. Does NOT follow this pattern. Deciding is the prerequisite of the other three: Character first decides intuitively *which* function to engage with the Objective. Deciding precedes the others (p. 284). Hence "primum mobile".

### Detail per Mental Factor (pp. 288-298)

**SENSING / INTENDING (pp. 288-294)**
> "Sensing — sensory perception through the five senses, revealed in our movements as weight; either light or strong." ("Book" p. 5)

Senses imagined as a two-way radio — receiving AND transmitting. Active mode: hearing → listening, looking → seeing. Sensing must NOT be contaminated by emotional or intellectual associations — keep it 'pure'. The danger is "skimming" sensory data into cerebral categories ("apples"). Vershinin's "Half my life for a glass of tea" is given as an example of vivid Sensing where Masha and Irma only cerebrally classify "evening tea".

> "All our senses work on the basis of an interface between specific body tissues and the environment. Thus all senses can be said to be a form of 'touch' in the widest sense. The reaction of our sensory tissues to this 'touch' is a form of counter-pressure, measured, ultimately, in terms of gravity. Hence Laban's association of Sensing with Weight." (p. 292)

> "Intending — stimulating the mind purposefully, to motivate an inner attitude or externalized drive, or to activate an action; revealed in the motion factor of weight and based upon the mental factor of sensing." ("Book" p. 5, q. p. 293)

The "menu in a restaurant" image (p. 293-294): we read the dishes purposefully → our Intending arouses → we order. "The stimulus for action is not the object itself, but the 'idea', the contact aroused internally by the object."

**THINKING / ATTENDING (pp. 295-298)**
> "Thinking — formation of ideas through intellectual, reflective reasoning, revealed in our movements as space; either flexible or direct." ("Book" p. 5)

Image: the Moorish fountains at Alhambra. Thinking = drifting reflection, NOT problem-solving. The lecturer-listener distinction: those who "twist inwardly under the influence of 'concave', doubting, questioning thinking" are Thinking; note-takers are Sensing (p. 295).

> "Thinking is linked by Laban with Space and Movement in Space. The movement of thought draws patterns in the soft wax of our minds — it is a journey between ideas, which takes either a direct or a convoluted, flexible route. To relate to an idea is to move closer to it; to reject it is to distance oneself from it." (p. 296)

Key division (p. 296): "Thinking 'in the clouds' is radically different from 'solving the problem of the play' — Attending; or from taking attitudes towards the issues raised by the play — Deciding." So Thinking = drift/contemplation; Attending = problem-solving; Deciding = attitude-taking. They are NOT synonyms.

> "ATTENDING — turning the mind tentatively, to motivate an inner attitude or externalized drive, or to activate an Action; revealed in the motion factor of space and based upon the mental factor of thinking." ("Book" p. 5, q. p. 297)

Hamlet's "To be or not to be", Launcelot Gobbo, Edmund's soliloquy = examples of tentative dual-pole questioning. Greta Garbo's "judging tentatively" = the iconic Attending face. Crucially (p. 298): "we often confuse Thinking and Attending with quick, direct, unhesitating decision taking." — they are NOT that.

(Wigman/Laban quarrel footnote, p. 296: Wigman thought Sensing/Weight was primary; Laban thought Thinking was the "first principle" of Movement. Malmgren puts Feeling first.)

## 6. Yielding vs. Contending

The 4-axis structure from the Grand Equation (p. 276):

| Mental Factor | Inner Participation | Motion Factor | Yielding | Contending | Negative | Quest |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| sensing | intending | weight | light | strong | heavy | what? |
| thinking | attending | space | flexible | direct | adrift | where? |
| intuiting | deciding | time | sustained | quick | indecisive | when? |
| feeling | adapting | flow | free | bound | irrelated | why? |

Yielding = "absorb the impact of the outside" / give in to the dimension; Contending = "exert ourselves on the world" / fight against the dimension (p. 313).

> "Laban considers that we take one of these fundamental attitudes towards each of the Motion Factors: we either yield with or contend against Weight, Space, Time and Flow." (p. 313)

Day vs night image (p. 314): "By day we are involved in the world, by night we 'retire' from it. We cannot be both 'receptive' and 'attacking' at the same time… characters are predominantly either active (contending) or relaxed (yielding)."

Sequence (p. 314): "yielding is the primordial, 'natural' state. We start an experience in a yielding state, we then increase its intensity into a contending state, before recovering back to yielding." Hence the "Book" lists yielding before contending.

Mythological framing from Laban (footnote p. 313): yielding gods float over the waters; "malign gods of death and violence are figures represented with thrusting, piercing and compressing effort actions, all of which are firm and direct movements".

### Detail per Mental Factor — continued

**INTUITING / DECIDING (pp. 299-303)**
> "Intuiting — insight by the mind without reasoning, revealed in our movements as time, either sustained or quick." ("Book" p. 5)

The child-and-electric-ring example (p. 299-300): not instinct (which is innate) but intuition (formed by experiences driven into the unconscious). "the present is matched, subliminally, with the past."

Stanislavski's "Magic If" works through Intuiting (p. 301): connects past to future without reference to present. Endowment (Uta Hagen's term) — attributing imaginary qualities to stage objects = Intuiting.

> "Deciding — the intuitive relating of the past and future with the present, to motivate an inner attitude or externalized drive, or to activate an action; revealed in the motion factor of time and based upon the mental factor of intuiting." ("Book" p. 5, q. p. 301)

> "Deciding is the catalyst which allows the other three Inner Participations to function and reveal themselves. Unlike the other three Inner Participations, Deciding cannot manifest itself on its own. It needs to combine with one of the others to become manifest." (p. 302)

Two kinds of decisions in Odysseus/Philoctetes example (p. 303):
- (a) creates character's memories of place + transformation of stage space
- (b) connects two image-sets across the 10-year gap

**FEELING / ADAPTING (pp. 304-309)**
> "Feeling — the emotion of liking and disliking, revealed in our movements as flow; either free or bound." ("Book" p. 5)

Free Feeling (liking) scatters/opens; Bound Feeling (dislike) gathers/contracts. Affects joints/articulations. Importantly: NOT to be confused with sensory like/dislike (peaches vs onions). "Feeling is about why, not about what we like and dislike" (p. 305) — emphasised. Carpenter (Conflict and Harmony Ch.5) quoted: "We have gone so far down this road that we have become a 'Where? What? and When?' people who hardly ever ask 'Why?'."

> "We usually think that emotions are the product of the situations in which we find ourselves. In reality, says Yat Malmgren, emotions are products of the 'Idea' in whose light we interpret the situations." (p. 305)

The audition/tennis example: bodily Bound Feeling symptoms = anticipation of an idea, not the event. After winning: Free Feeling — chest swells, heart soars.

Ideas which induce Feeling operate in two phases (p. 305):
1. character surrenders entirely to an idea
2. having adapted, character constructs a new reality with its help

> "All fundamental motivations (Super-Objectives), says Yat Malmgren, are ultimately emotional in nature and therefore emerge from the Mental Factor of Feeling." (p. 306)

> "Adapting — the extraverted adjustment of one's Self to the outer world, and the introverted relating of one's conscious Self with the subconscious; revealed in the motion factor of flow and based on the mental factor of feeling." ("Book" p. 6, q. p. 307)

Berenice vs Cleopatra (p. 308): both oriental queens facing Roman conquest. Berenice yields → Free Adapting; Cleopatra fights → Bound Adapting (anger). Same Given Circumstances; different inner self-images → different Adapting → different Action.

### Motion Factors — detail per axis (pp. 315-326)

The four-ingredients-of-a-car analogy (p. 315): Flow = oil, Space = water, Weight = petrol, Time = gears (accelerate/decelerate). Character type = proportion of these.

**WEIGHT (Sensing/Intending) — Light vs Strong (pp. 316-318)**

> "The dynamic motion factor which expresses the Mental Factor of Sensing and the Inner Participation of Intending; the impact of receiving and transmitting sensory stimuli — subdivided into the Elements of Light and Strong." ("Book" p. 11)

> "Light Intending — a light, sensory, muscular exertion which does not involve contraction of the central body muscles." ("Book" p. 6)
> "Strong Intending — a sensory muscular firmness in any part of the body, and emanating from the contraction of the central body muscles." ("Book" p. 6)

Carpenter footnote on the spectrum (p. 316): "Relaxation of the body or of its parts causing fall or collapse leads to inertness. Exaggerated tensions causing tautness and cramp leads to rigidity. The undivided fight between near cramp and near collapse results in **Heaviness**. Only Light and Strong are fully controllable Weight qualities." So Heaviness = the unresolvable cramp-vs-collapse fight = the Weight Negative.

Light is primitive/initial. Sequence: Light → Strong (cross border) → recover Light. Failure to recover = "muscle-bound", tense, rigid.

Resistance can be internal (antagonistic muscles) OR external (obstacles in the play). Hence: a Light Character with a Strong Action and vice versa is possible. By extension same applies to all four Motion Factors. (p. 317)

> "We can therefore have a Light Character with a Strong Action and vice-versa." (p. 317)

Physically expressed in **height**: Light = upwards (lift away from gravity); Strong = downwards. (p. 318) Physical centre = abdominal muscles (linked to 12 dorsal vertebrae). Strong Intending contracts them; Light Intending does not.

Examples: Sir Toby Belch, Othello = high Weight (sensuous warmth, large stage volume). Aguecheek, Roderigo, Cassius = lack Weight (fay, weak, untrustworthy).

**SPACE (Thinking/Attending) — Flexible vs Direct (pp. 318-322)**

> "The kinetic Motion Factor which expresses the Mental Factor of Thinking and the Inner Participation of Attending; the reflection of thoughtful movements in one or more planes of space — subdivided into the elements of Flexible and Direct." ("Book" p. 11)

> "Flexible Attending — a reflective movement which is roundabout, being both concave and convex in two or more planes of space."
> "Direct Attending — an intentive movement which traverses either a straight line, or a flat curve, in a single plane of space." ("Book" p. 6)

Laban's "kinesphere" — bubble around the body that travels with us; division between intimate and general space. (p. 319)

**Direct vs Flexible mechanics (p. 321):**
- Direct Attending: operates only on one side of the body at a time (left- or right-sided expression). Linear. Two planes of space. Sharp, powerful, "intentive" (eager, earnest, penetrating).
- Flexible Attending: crosses the vertical centre-line of the body. Three or more planes of space. Energy never reaches full intensity (loses impetus on the convoluted trajectory). Tentative, half-realised. "Reflective" — convoluted, hesitant, circumspect.
- Circular thought is Direct, NOT Flexible (one plane only).

**The Diagonals of Space** (p. 321-322): Stage as cube crisscrossed by 4 diagonals meeting at the centre. Actor placed in centre, 4 diagonals in front, 4 behind. Contacts/objectives sit on these diagonals. Energy flows out (extravert thought) or in (introvert thought). For each contact direction (e.g. Up Right), the introvert "recovery" sphere lies on the opposite end of the diagonal (Down Left). Direct moves: shortest route. Flexible moves: roundabout path on/around the diagonals.

Hamlet's "To be or not to be" mode example (p. 320): hands tap, feet tap (extremities — Atlas/Axis sphere), trunk unaffected (Attending pushes Intending out from centre to extremities). Energy is concave ("To be" — moves toward the idea) then convex ("or not to be" — withdraws toward the *néant*).

**TIME (Intuiting/Deciding) — Sustained vs Quick (pp. 322-325)**

> "The rhythmic Motion Factor which expresses the Mental Factor of Intuiting and the Inner Participation of Deciding; the intuitive perception of the relation between the past and the future — sub-divided into the elements of Sustained and Quick." ("Book" p. 11)

> "Sustained Deciding — an intuitive clinging to the past."
> "Quick Deciding — an intuitive urge to the future." ("Book" p. 6)

Carpenter (Conflict and Harmony Ch.4, footnoted p. 322): "In our practical **Working Actions** Time is the co-ordinating factor between Weight and Space." Working Actions confirmed as a discrete concept. Carpenter splits "intuitive time" from chronometric time — qualitative vs quantitative.

**Time is all-pervasive** (p. 323-324): Yat Malmgren says we should speak of THREE tempi, not one:
- **Dynamic tempo** = sensation tempo
- **Intellectual tempo** = fast to-and-fro of thought
- **Emotional tempo** = slow flow of emotion through 'veins'

(This is a richer "physical tempo" than the Inner-Participation tempi above on p. 286.)

> "Time is neutral, says Yat Malmgren. Time is indifferent, heartless, lacks either Feeling or Sensation. It does, however, act as the catalyst for the other three Motion Factors." (p. 323) Confirming Time/Deciding's role as primum mobile.

Macbeth (Sustained — clings to past) vs Lady Macbeth (Quick — driven to future) example (p. 324). They swap at end. Konstantin (past-clinging) vs Nina (future-driven) in Seagull.

Time exists at TWO levels: Time at Character level looks to past (formative experiences); Time at Action level looks to future (Objective). They flow into each other on the figure-8.

Physically: Time on backward-forward axis. Sustained leans back; Quick thrusts forward. (p. 325)

**FLOW (Feeling/Adapting) — Free vs Bound (pp. 326-…)**

> "The cyclic Motion Factor which expresses the Mental Factor of Feeling and the Inner Participation of Adapting; the feeling of the viscosity of movement (expression) — sub-divided into the elements of Free and Bound." ("Book" p. 11)

> "Free Adapting — a feeling of streaming, unarrestable fluidity of movement."
> "Bound Adapting — a feeling in movement of sticky, viscid fluidity arrestable at any stage of development with a continuous readiness to stop." ("Book" p. 6)

Important reordering (p. 326): Although Laban's 'academic' order puts Flow LAST, "Yat Malmgren considers that Characters react to reality through Flow first." Adapting is the first response to disturbance — characters thrown off balance by impact of others and adapt to restore balance. Adapting determines Flow of inner energy and therefore movement. From outside Flow = the presence/absence of 'relaxation' or 'fluency' of movement.

### Important "Shadow Move" reference (footnote p. 292)

Carpenter (Conflict and Harmony Ch.3): "...there would seem to be no obvious connection between, shall we say, hearing or tasting with an unconscious **Shadow Move** resulting in a Strong or Light movement in some part of our body…"

So a Shadow Move = the unconscious bodily response to a sensory perception, resulting in a Light or Strong movement. The fish-in-aquarium example: passive Sense Perception → fish yields, peripheral lightness in tail/fins; active Sense Perception → "its whole body curves suddenly in a strong contending **slash** against Weight." Note the use of "slash" — a Working Action — to describe an automatic reflexive response. Suggests Shadow Moves and Working Actions are linked.

This confirms part of the user's hypothesis: Shadow Moves are involuntary (specifically unconscious) bodily responses on a Motion-Factor axis. Need to find Mirodan's own definition next.

> "The four factors of Weight, Space, Time and Flow into which all movement (expression) can be analysed, and which express the four Mental Factors of Sensing, Thinking, Intuiting, and Feeling (respectively). Each Motion Factor is sub-divided into two elements, which yield with or contend against the factors." ("Book" p. 2, q. p. 310)

(Words in brackets are Malmgren's addition to Carpenter's original.)

Laban's four observation-questions (p. 310):
- which body part moves and how does it co-ordinate?
- duration / relation to surrounding durations?
- muscular power exerted?
- direction in space?

Mapping (p. 311):
- Weight = physical expression of Intending (Sensing)
- Space = physical expression of Attending (Thinking)
- Time = physical expression of Deciding (Intuiting)
- Flow = physical expression of Adapting (Feeling)

CRITICAL Malmgren correction (p. 312): "the words Weight, Space, Time and Flow have no expressive power in themselves, he stresses — they are codes which describe a psychological experience… These terms are abstract conventions, like writing H2O for water." Don't separate Motion Factors from their Inner Participation roots.

Actor examples Malmgren uses to illustrate (p. 312): Olivier > Gielgud > Richardson on "Weight"; reverse on Flow. Othello = Weight (sensuality), Desdemona = Flow (emotion), Cassio = Flow (charm), Iago = Space (thinking).

The Elements table (p. 313):

| Inner Participation (Motion Factor) | Yielding | Contending |
|---|---|---|
| INTENDING (Weight) | Light | Strong |
| ATTENDING (Space) | Flexible | Direct |
| DECIDING (Time) | Sustained | Quick |
| ADAPTING (Flow) | Free | Bound |

Malmgren's preference (p. 314): the "Book" attaches Elements to Inner Participations rather than to Motion Factors — "Othello's Intending or Desdemona's Adapting (not their Weight and Flow) are their dominant features." In practice both work, since Inner Participation can only be observed via Motion Factor.

## 7. Working Actions (the eight)

The chapter heading is **"WORKING ACTIONS and SUBCONSCIOUS MOTIFS"** — these are paired concepts.

> "The eight basic actions, in which all consciously performed activities can be analysed. Compounded of the Motion Factors of Weight, Space, Time and 'industrial' Free Flow (co-ordination)." ("Book" p. 7, q. p. 340)

Note: Carpenter's original Glossary said "consciously performed *functional* activities". Malmgren DROPS "functional" because Working Actions apply to BOTH psychological and physical activities. He also adds "industrial" and "Free" to "Flow" — emphasising that the Flow involved is mechanical co-ordination, not real psychological Free Flow.

> "The combinations of three Elements — one from each Motion Factor — create eight basic types of physical movement which Laban calls the Working Actions." (p. 340)

Why three not four? Because real Feeling (Free Flow) is NOT present in physical Working Actions — Flow acts as the body's "recovery zone" between Working Actions (Carpenter footnote, p. 340): "the reason why we do not use more than three of the four Motion Factors in our working actions… man's sanity is safeguarded by an inherent rhythm of effort and recovery… only in states of ecstasy or in drunkenness when man's controls are over-ridden, can an action be observed which is compounded of all the four Motion Factors."

So a Working Action is a 3-axis combination on Weight × Space × Time, with industrial-Free-Flow as the always-present 4th. Flow proper is the recovery zone BETWEEN actions.

The eight (p. 341), in order from most yielding to most contending:

| Working Action | Weight | Space | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| FLOATING | light | flexible | sustained |
| FLICKING | light | flexible | quick |
| GLIDING | light | direct | sustained |
| DABBING | light | direct | quick |
| WRINGING | strong | flexible | sustained |
| SLASHING | strong | flexible | quick |
| PRESSING | strong | direct | sustained |
| PUNCHING | strong | direct | quick |

(Floating = pure yielding on all three. Punching = pure contending on all three.)

Laban's pairing rule (footnote p. 341): each Working Action has exactly ONE contrast (the diametrically opposed Action — Floating ↔ Punching, etc.). Three other Actions differ in one element; three differ in two. So the eight form a tight 3-bit lattice.

### Detailed descriptions per Working Action (pp. 342-345)

Each is given with a tune (Malmgren's mnemonic), a physical activity, typical transitive verbs, and Laban's mythological characterisation:

**FLOATING (Light/Flexible/Sustained)** — p. 342
- Tune: none — "so 'disembodied'… no tune can truly express it"
- Physical: like wrapping oneself in a gossamer veil
- Verbs: to enfold, to envelop, to nuzzle, to shroud
- Pure Floating cannot be achieved physically; only emotion/thought can pure-Float. Body carried passively on a tide.
- Laban's gods: "Gods floating over the waters show in ritual or pictorial representations a yielding attitude towards the motion factors of Time, Weight and Space."

**FLICKING (Light/Flexible/Quick)** — p. 342
- Tune: "Jingle Bells"
- Physical: sprinkling, sowing
- Verbs: to tweak, to spin
- "Did I?" or "All right?" — voice habit. Flames in an open fire.
- Laban: "The glittering divinities of joy and surprise… sensation of lightness wedded to indulging in space."

**GLIDING (Light/Direct/Sustained)** — p. 343
- Tune: "Valse" from Tchaikovsky's "Sleeping Beauty"
- Physical: ice-skating, wind-surfing, curling
- Verbs: to lead, to skim, to stroke, to slide
- Laban: "essentially a sustained and direct movement with gentle touch. In gliding man and his deity are enveloped in the experience of infinity of time and the cessation of the drag of weight, but they are actively concerned with the directional clarity of their movements."

**DABBING (Light/Direct/Quick)** — p. 343
- Tune: "Puppet on a String"; "Two Ladies" in Cabaret
- Physical: knocking on door, pointillism, woodpecker
- Verbs: to dot, to nibble, to snip, to waggle
- "An exact, punctilious energy."
- Laban: "Sprites and goblins… sudden and direct and yet gentle."

**WRINGING (Strong/Flexible/Sustained)** — p. 343
- Tune: "Nobody Knows The Trouble I Have Seen"
- Physical: wringing water from a towel
- Verbs: to gouge, to twist
- "Never stops" — like the turn of a vice. Twisting spiral. Mary Wigman's exercise of being 'screwed into the floor', then 'unscrewing' out.
- Laban: "Wringing gods' movements would be flexible and speak of the gradual passage of time, yet they would be strong and firm."

**SLASHING (Strong/Flexible/Quick)** — p. 344
- Tune: "La Marseillaise"
- Physical: vigorous duster, whipping cream by hand
- Verbs: to flail, to scourge, to swipe, to whip
- Three planes of space, like a coach driver's whip.
- Laban: "Slashing gods who would fight against time with swiftness and powerful resistance and yet be flexible in space."

**PRESSING (Strong/Direct/Sustained)** — p. 344
- Tune: "Volga, Volga"
- Physical: ironing, planing wood, working metal on a lathe
- Verbs: to bend, to compress, to crease, to drill

**PUNCHING (Strong/Direct/Quick)** — p. 344
- Tune: "Rule Britannia"
- Physical: axing, hammering
- Verbs: to box, to butt, to chop, to shove, to stab
- Laban (covers both Pressing and Punching): "The malign gods of death and violence are figures represented with thrusting, piercing and compressing effort actions… sometimes occurring suddenly and at other times gradually." Sudden = Punching; gradual = Pressing.

### Working Action — direction, pulse, hierarchy (pp. 345-348)

**Push or pull** (p. 345): Each Working Action's energy can flow toward OR away from the mover. A Pressing can be a push or a pull. The energy has a "pulse" like a musical beat — needs an asymmetric weighting (push out + relaxation in, OR swing in + relaxation out).

**Degrees of stress** — Carpenter's quantitative measure (p. 346): under-stressed / normal / stressed / over-stressed. With these "there are 64 variants of each of eight Working Actions, making a total of 512." So the eight are anchors, not bins.

**Internal hierarchy / preparation rule** (p. 347 — IMPORTANT):
> "We cannot carry out a Punching action without first undergoing the inner preparation of Pressing. If I take an axe to chop wood, I first Press to lift the axe above my head and only then release the Punch of the blow. This order cannot be reversed."

So each contending (Quick) Working Action requires preparation in its Sustained partner. Slashing requires Wringing-prep. Dabbing requires Gliding-prep. Punching requires Pressing-prep. If you skip the prep, the inner Light becomes a Flick instead of a Slash.

**Introvert (Preparation) ↔ Extravert (Execution)** pairing (p. 348):

| Introvert (Preparation) | → | Extravert (Execution) |
|---|---|---|
| Floating | → | Flicking |
| Gliding | → | Dabbing |
| Wringing | → | Slashing |
| Pressing | → | Punching |

The Sustained Working Actions ARE the inner preparation; the Quick ones ARE the outer execution. (Carpenter's note p. 348: pressing/wringing/floating/gliding have a sense of past; punching/slashing/dabbing/flicking have a sense of future.)

**Continuous transitions** (p. 348-349): "transitions which involve the change of only one Element are smooth and perceived as 'realistic'. Those which change two or even three Elements are big acting jumps which 'shock' the audience." Inexperienced actors love Punching↔Floating jumps; skilled actors use the full scale. Like piano keys.

## 8. Subconscious Motifs / Physical Tempo / Shadow Moves — vocabulary

This is the section the user wanted clarified. Mirodan's exact distinctions (pp. 349-355):

### SUBCONSCIOUS MOTIFS (defined p. 349)

> "Laban and Malmgren consider that the inner impulse has the same shape as the outer movement and therefore are analysed through the Elements of Inner Participations in the same way that Working Actions can be analysed through the Elements of Motion Factors. However, to emphasize the psychological nature of the inner impulse, and to avoid confusion in the terminology, these inner counterparts of the Working Actions are called SUBCONSCIOUS MOTIFS." (p. 349)

So: a **Subconscious Motif** is the INNER (psychological) version of a Working Action. They share names (a Pressing Subconscious Motif → a Pressing Working Action). The Working Action is the outer 3-axis Motion-Factor cocktail; the Subconscious Motif is the inner 3-axis Inner-Participation cocktail (Intending × Attending × Deciding) — same pattern, different layer.

Piano analogy: "no one can make music at the piano without calling into play both the inner movement of energy and the outer physical exertion" (p. 350). Subconscious Motif = inner; Working Action = outer.

### Working Actions in voice and characterisation

Working Actions are NOT just gestures — they are HEARD in voice as much as seen in the body (p. 349). A Slashing can come out as a winding, insistent, drawn "go on!". Plus the Inner Working Action is "coloured" by inner Flow: Bound Flow makes a Punch aggressive; Free Flow makes it loving/desiring.

Transitive-verb principle (pp. 350-351): Working Actions are abstractions; what an actor PLAYS is a transitive verb that *fits* a Working Action. "Punching" can't be acted. "To box" can. Some verbs map to one Working Action ("to snip" = Dabbing only); others are double/triple/quadruple. "To hammer" cannot be Dabbing — "if you were the nail, you would not like to have the action done to you." (p. 351)

Whole characters by dominant Working Action / tempo (p. 352):
- Mincing in *The Way of the World* — a Dabbing character (quick, direct, light, pretentious-affectation).
- Antony in *Julius Caesar* — primarily Wringing (winds his way around others).
- Animal comparisons ("sparrow-like", "squirrel-like") because animals have clear-cut dominant tempi.

### Physical Tempo — what it actually is

Mirodan does NOT use "Physical Tempo" as a stand-alone capitalised term in the chapter. The closest concepts are:

- **Tempo** at p. 286 — Inner Participations expressed in overall tempo (Attending = fast, Intending = medium, Adapting = slow). Tempo at this level is the physical realisation of Deciding.
- **Three tempi** at p. 323-324 — Time is all-pervasive, so we should speak of dynamic / intellectual / emotional tempo (one per Mental Factor that lives in time).
- **Working-Action tempo** at pp. 351-352 — each Working Action / transitive verb has a "definite tempo" ("to mock" vs "to chastise" vs "to patronise"). Characters defined by dominant Working Action ARE defined by their dominant tempo.

So "physical tempo" in Malmgren = the Working-Action signature of the character. Pace, rhythm, and Working-Action are inseparable. Tempo is the audible/visible trace of Deciding (Time/Intuiting) modulating the other functions.

### SHADOW MOVES (pp. 354-355) — DEFINITIVE

The user's hypothesis was wrong. Shadow Moves are NOT axis-pairs. They are **involuntary unconscious gestures**.

> "Movements by any part of the body performed without conscious volition expressing Inner Attitudes and Externalized Drives." ("Glossary" p. 4 and "Book" 1970s p. 9, q. p. 355)

(Note: this definition is in Mirodan's quoted text but "for some reason does not feature in the latest version of Yat Malmgren's 'Book'." — footnote p. 355.)

Mirodan's framing (p. 354):
> "We have assumed until now that the inner life of a character is only revealed through conscious moves in Action. Laban shows however, that there is an exception to this rule: the Character can 'speak' directly through unconscious and involuntary moves."

> "I sometimes think of the inner nature of a character as of a primitive organism, a worm or an amoeba. When 'touched' it reacts, vibrating almost imperceptibly as it re-arranges the Elements of its Inner Participations. This inner movement of energy produces involuntary outer motions, 'half-gestures' with no apparent function other than to reflect and release the inner tensions of the Character. A scratch of the head, a flick of the wrist, a narrowing of the eyes and a myriad similar moves have no functional purpose, but communicate to others the inner reaction." (p. 354)

Carpenter (Conflict & Harmony Ch.1 p.2, footnoted p. 355):
> "Our gestures, mannerisms and bodily attitudes are, however, activated by our subconscious minds and have been aptly christened 'Shadow Moves' by Rudolf Laban since they have no immediate practical purpose. Of these we are normally as unaware as we are of our sun-shadow; they are the visible language of a level of our mind which lies below consciousness."

Examples (Laban): "tiny muscular movements such as the raising of the brow, the jerking of the hand or the tapping of the foot, which have none other than expressive value. They are usually done unconsciously and often accompany movements of purposeful action like a shadow."

**Crucial mechanic** (p. 355):
> "They are generated directly by the Subconscious Motifs. A Pressing Subconscious Motif, for example, is not a push or pull against an external obstacle, but an inner holding, a contraction of the central body muscles. A Punching Subconscious Motif is also directed inwardly, as an inner 'kick' against or towards the Objective which gave it birth. It is this inner 'holding', 'kicking', 'slashing', etc., which comes out in Shadow Moves."

Etymology — Jungian (footnote p. 354): "Shadow" comes from Jung. In strict Jungian usage Shadow = inferior/repressed self ("Mr Hyde to our Dr Jekyll"). Laban/Carpenter use it more loosely — "a hidden part of the psyche, not necessarily evil… which the ego does not wish to see revealed through its conscious actions."

Why Malmgren teaches them lightly (footnote p. 355): "Yat Malmgren does not discuss the Shadow Moves much in his teaching, presumably because these occur in acting as automatic by-products of an inner state and therefore, while their existence can be signalled, they cannot be taught or practised consciously."

### Chain of revelation — definitive

Putting it together:
1. Mental Factor (latent) — Sensing/Thinking/Intuiting/Feeling
2. catalysed by external Objective →
3. Inner Participation (active) — Intending/Attending/Deciding/Adapting
4. cocktailed in 3-axis combination →
5. **Subconscious Motif** (inner, named after the 8 Working Actions) ←—— this generates BOTH:
   6a. **Working Actions** = outer, conscious, functional, transitive-verb-namable activities
   6b. **Shadow Moves** = outer, involuntary, non-functional, unconscious gestures (raising brow, tapping foot, jerking hand)

So Working Actions and Shadow Moves are the TWO outward expressions of the same inner Subconscious Motif:
- Working Action = volitional, has purpose, can be planned, can be acted via transitive verbs
- Shadow Move = involuntary, no purpose, unplanned, communicates the inner state directly

This is the user's key vocabulary clarification: Shadow Moves are NOT axis-pairs ("Strong & Direct"). They are the unconscious twitches/gestures that reveal a Subconscious Motif without the mediation of conscious action.

### Shadow Moves — additional mechanics (pp. 356-358)

> "Unlike Working Actions, Shadow Moves cannot be fully analysed into Motion Factors because they are incomplete moves, often used as 'steps' between one Working Action and another. They are incomplete in the sense that they are deficient in one or other of the Motion Factors." (p. 356)

So a Shadow Move is **a 2-axis fragment** (one Motion Factor missing) — not a 3-axis Working Action. They are "steps" between Working Actions. They reveal "unstressed" / underdeveloped Inner Participations of the character.

Carpenter: audience perception is "quick to recognize an inconsistent movement" — primitive nervous-system inheritance flags inconsistent movement as the threat-of-an-enemy signal. (p. 356)

Paulina (Seagull) example (p. 356-357): waves her hand / shrugs / "It doesn't matter" = incomplete "step" gestures BETWEEN Light Slashing and Flicking. Both away-from-centre = away-from-emotional-centre. Reveals: her Adapting is undeveloped; she leans on Intending. Hidden conflict between her Sensuous nature and her Feeling-centred outer Action — Laban: "an incongruous interplay of rhythms and shapes indicative of conflict between a character's inner attitude and his outer demeanour."

Critical (p. 357):
> "Shadow Moves always reveal the hidden nature and never the acknowledged intentions of the character. Paulina's Super-Objective in the play (the 'Through-Action' of which the character herself is aware) may be defined by the Character as 'to be loved', but the actress can see that at a deeper, hidden level it is closer to 'to control'."

The Shadow Move betrays the secret motivation while the verbal Action gives "lip service" to the open wish.

Stanislavski tempo-rhythm linkage (p. 357): outer tempo controlled, inner turmoil leaks through tremble of lip / shaking knee. "Catch him suddenly off guard… for a second or two he will start, jump up and run towards you in the rapid rhythm of what was going on inside him." When that happens the Shadow Moves become full Working Actions (3 axes display) and a NEW set of minute Shadow Moves emerges — character now needs to conceal the new state. The Shadow-Move/Working-Action relationship is "extremely fluid, reflecting the fluid relationship between consciousness and the unconscious." (p. 358)

### THE EFFORT CUBE (pp. 359-364)

The Effort Cube is Laban's spatial graphic of how Motion Factors map to body axes:

- **Weight (Intending)** moves on **height** axis. Strong = down, Light = up. Divided by the **'table' plane** (horizontal centre).
- **Space (Attending)** moves on **width** axis. Direct vs Flexible. Divided by the **'door' plane** (vertical front-back). Flexible/Direct sides depend on whether the action is right-handed or left-handed (extravert vs introvert side of the kinesphere).
- **Time (Deciding)** moves on **depth** axis. Quick = forward, Sustained = backward. Divided by the **'wheel' plane** (vertical sideways).

So the cube has 3 perpendicular axes — Weight=height, Space=width, Time=depth. The 8 corners = the 8 Working Actions. E.g., Punching (Strong/Direct/Quick) at Right/Deep/Forward (RDF) on a right-handed action; Floating (Light/Flexible/Sustained) at Left/High/Backward (LHB).

**Flow does NOT have an axis** — Flow is portrayed as a "cloud" pervading the cube (p. 361). It colours/informs movement, like a gas or liquid. The cube represents "the Character's capacity to DO, regardless of his emotional state."

Working Action relations within the cube (p. 362): same-Element pairs link organically — Wringing turns into Floating along the table plane (same Space and Time, only Weight changes). Pressing↔Gliding, Punching↔Dabbing, similarly. The cube can be "read" along Weight, Space, or Time axes.

**Critical Malmgren departure** (p. 362-363):
> "Yat Malmgren's cube 'sits' inside the actor's body. Its positions are not to be taken only as places where a Working Action can be carried out physically. They are also imaginative locations of Subconscious Motifs which are more often than not expressed in acting through the voice ('I will do it!' — Punch), accompanied by unconscious gestures (Shadow Moves)."

So Malmgren's Effort Cube is INSIDE the actor, not surrounding him. Floating LHB: "Oh, well…" → eyes and head spiral in a Shadow Move toward the LHB point.

> "We are thus presented with two uses of the cube: Laban's original graphics, representing physical movement, are properly described as an Action cube, while Yat Malmgren's inner cube is actually a Character Cube." (p. 363)

A character can have his own particular cube, pinpointing the intensity and position of his Subconscious Motifs.

Sphere caveat (p. 363): "actors are more likely to conceive their inner space as a sphere within which the inner arouses 'concave' images and sensations, while the outer is perceived as 'convex'." But cube is retained for the labelling clarity.

Two cubes per character — one for right-action, one for left-action — because effort always works on a diagonal toward an Objective placed in opposition. (p. 363)

**Colour-coding** (p. 364):
- **Weight (Sensing/Intending)** = RED — aggressive, blood, warmth, sensuality
- **Space (Thinking/Attending)** = BLUE — cool, sky, reason, detachment
- **Flow (Feeling/Adapting)** = GREEN — fickle, chlorophyll, the sea
- **Time (Intuiting/Deciding)** = WHITE — combination of the other three (red+blue+green = white in light spectrum); Time is the prerequisite to the existence of the others

Shading: Light Intending = pale pink, Strong Intending = full blood-red.

End of Chapter I (printed p. 364).

## 8. Subconscious Motifs / Physical Tempo / Shadow Moves — vocabulary

The Working Actions section is titled "WORKING ACTIONS and SUBCONSCIOUS MOTIFS" (p. 340) — they are introduced as a pair. Need to extract the next chunk to confirm Subconscious Motif definitions and Shadow Moves vs Working Actions distinction.

## 9. Negatives

From the Grand Equation (p. 276): four Negatives map 1:1 to the four Motion Factor axes.

Negatives originate in a failure of **Adapting** — characters who fail to absorb a disappointment / trauma. The "creative give and take between the 'I' and the object ceases to flow." Their psychological functions freeze. Negatives are a "neutral" state — half-way house between Yielding and Contending where the *interplay* is suspended (p. 333).

KEY CLARIFICATION: "in Negative states, the interplay between the Elements is cancelled, NOT the Elements themselves" (p. 334). So a Heavy character is aware he ought to be Strong-Intending but cannot summon the energy. "I just can't, I can't be bothered."

**HEAVY** — Negative of Intending (Weight) (pp. 333-334)
> "The negatively neutral and impotent quality of Weight (Intending) in which the interplay of Light and Strong is cancelled by an inertia which negates the receiving and transmitting intentions of Sensing." ("Book" p. 14)

Carpenter's note: "The undivided fight between near cramp and near collapse results in Heaviness." Depressed people: beard grows, dishes pile up. Tennessee Williams characters — Blanche du Bois takes baths constantly because circulation has frozen.

**ADRIFT** — Negative of Attending (Space) (p. 335)
> "The negatively neutral quality of Space in which the interplay of Flexible and Direct is cancelled by a disorientation which negates the reflective (concave) and attending (convex) aspects of Thinking." ("Book" p. 14)

First seen in a blank expression of the eyes. Bemused, lost in a labyrinth. The pensioner attending British Museum lectures "for something to do" — hearing but not listening, neither doubting nor accepting.

**INDECISIVE** — Negative of Deciding (Time) (pp. 335-336)
> "The negatively neutral dream quality of Time in which the interplay of Sustained and Quick is cancelled by a timelessness which negates the relating of past, present and future in the decisions of Intuiting." ("Book" p. 14)

Connected with Adrift. Floats around, no past memories, no future hopes. Affects "dreamy" expression thinking it makes him interesting, but in reality it makes him "plain stupid." Naive characters Roderigo, Aguecheek often Indecisive. The 1920s "Sphinx-eye" makeup fashion = trying to fake Indecisive as mysterious depth.

**IRRELATED** — Negative of Adapting (Flow) (p. 336)
> "The negatively neutral and frozen quality of Flow in which the interplay of Free and Bound is cancelled by an emotional fixity which negates the extraverted and introverted aspects of the adapting and relating of Feeling." ("Book" p. 14)

"Why should I bother to take into account things which do not touch me directly? I wasn't murdered, I wasn't the one they robbed — these things do not affect me!" Emotional deep freeze. The proverbial commuter reading his paper on the train: doesn't Sense the murders, doesn't Think about the politics, doesn't Decide anything, doesn't Adapt.

(Note: Carpenter calls this "frigid" in the original — gendered language; "impotent" for Heavy. Mirodan flags this as period-bound, points out Negatives apply regardless of gender.)

## 10. Inner Quests

From the Grand Equation (p. 276) and the Inner Quests section (pp. 337-339):

> "What? asked of... Intending"
> "Where? asked of... Attending"
> "When? asked of... Deciding"
> "Why? asked of... Adapting"
> ("Book" p. 3)

The Quests "represent the mental journey undertaken by the movement analyst from the observation of someone's involuntary gestures to the understanding of their significance in terms of the personality of the mover. The actor reverses this process and undertakes a journey from psychological characteristics outwards towards physical gesture." (p. 337)

Yielding meditation angles for the actor (p. 337):
- **What?**: material nature, social background, circumstances
- **Where?**: direction, geographical location (country, city, house, room)
- **When?**: duration, period, season, time of day
- **Why?**: motivation, especially emotional roots

Carpenter's full matrix (p. 338) — Yielding "Quest" / Latent middle / Contending "Answer":

| Inner Participation | Basic Quest | Question | Latent | Answer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intending | What? | Which? | This | That! |
| Attending | Where? | Yonder? | Here | There! |
| Deciding | When? | Has Been? | Now | Will be! |
| Adapting | Why? | You? | None | I! |

Yat Malmgren himself does NOT use the "Questions, Answers, Latent" columns in the "Book" or his teaching — only the basic Quests. (Footnote p. 338)

"The system charts the flow of psychological energy into physical expression under the impact of a Super-Objective. The Super-Objective intervenes at the level of the Inner Quests." (Diagram caption near p. 276)

Function: "The Quests form part of the actor's work method and their primary function is to release creative answers from the unconscious. They act as an irritant, provoking the emergence of a latent Mental Factor into an active Inner Participation." (p. 339) The questioning function is the same as in Oedipus / Parsifal Jungian quest myths — "the Grail's redemption depends on the right question being asked".

## 11. Surprises / overrides

- Effort is a SEQUENCE not a thing — a process, "the battery that moves the hand of the clock" (p. 275). Don't think of Efforts as discrete states.
- Malmgren TWICE rearranges the canonical Jungian function order. The "Book" uses sensing/thinking/intuiting/feeling (academic). Malmgren in practice teaches feeling/sensing/intuiting/thinking (organic) because emotional-relaxation-first is the prerequisite for everything. (Interview 4.6.93)
- Mental Factors are PASSIVE / LATENT. Only activated by external catalyst into Inner Participations. The actor cannot will pure energy; he must engage with given circumstances. (p. 282)
- "Volume" / "presence" = the sum of how developed the four Mental Factors are. Actor "is long before he does anything" (p. 279). This is the system's account of star quality.
- Olivier "never hit the roof with the volume but always left some room" (p. 278) — a Malmgren-told anecdote framing star presence as conscious *control* of the four Mental Factors.
- **Deciding (Intuiting/Time) is primum mobile.** It does not have a "toward / away from" valence (p. 284). It pre-decides which of the other three functions to engage. Tempo IS Deciding made physical (p. 286). Don't treat the four functions as symmetric.
- **Subconscious Motif vs Working Action — a non-obvious split.** The Subconscious Motif IS the inner 3-axis cocktail of Inner Participations; the Working Action is the outer 3-axis cocktail of Motion Factors. They share names. The Working Action "is more often heard in the inflections of the voice than seen in gestures" (p. 349) — voice carries inner Working Actions as much as body does.
- **Working Actions only use 3 of the 4 Motion Factors.** Real Flow is excluded because the brain/body needs Flow as recovery zone. Only "industrial Free Flow" is co-present. A 4-Motion-Factor action only happens in ecstasy or drunkenness (p. 340). This is structural, not aesthetic.
- **Working Actions have a strict preparation hierarchy.** Punching requires Pressing-prep first; Slashing requires Wringing-prep; Dabbing requires Gliding-prep; Flicking requires Floating-prep (p. 347-348). If you skip the preparation, the action degrades into its lighter prep-version. The introvert Sustained Working Actions ARE the inner preparation, the extravert Quick ones ARE the execution.
- **Shadow Moves are 2-axis fragments.** They cannot be analysed by the full Motion Factor triad — they are deficient in one Motion Factor (p. 356). They are "steps" between Working Actions. They reveal the *underdeveloped* (unstressed) Inner Participations of the character.
- **Shadow Moves contradict the conscious Action.** "Shadow Moves always reveal the hidden nature and never the acknowledged intentions of the character" (p. 357). They are the involuntary leak of the secret motivation while verbal Action gives "lip service" to the open wish. Paulina's "to be loved" verbally / "to control" via Shadow.
- **Negatives are NOT absences of axis activity.** The Elements remain — what fails is the *interplay* between Yielding and Contending (p. 334). The Heavy character is aware he ought to be Strong-Intending but cannot mobilise. Don't model Negatives as "the axis is off"; model them as "the axis is suspended in static equilibrium between its poles."
- **Light Character + Strong Action is allowed.** A character can yield on Weight while the play's obstacles force Strong Action (p. 317). Inner and outer can have opposing Elements on the SAME Motion Factor. By extension this applies to all four axes — character Element ≠ action Element.
- **Three tempi, not one.** Time is all-pervasive — every other Mental Factor lives in time (p. 323-324). A character has a dynamic tempo (sensing speed), an intellectual tempo (thinking speed), AND an emotional tempo (slow circulation of emotion). They can differ — Orson Welles fast-sensing/slow-emotion; Garbo the inverse.
- **Yielding precedes Contending in time.** "We start an experience in a yielding state, we then increase its intensity into a contending state, before recovering back to yielding" (p. 314). This is why the "Book" lists yielding first. The natural cycle is yield → contend → yield. Day vs night metaphor.
- **The Effort Cube has two readings: Action Cube (outside the actor, Laban's original) and Character Cube (inside the actor, Malmgren's contribution)** (p. 363). Same geometry, opposite orientation. Outside-the-body cube constricts; inside-the-body cube generates energy. Two cubes per character — one for right-action, one for left-action — because effort always works on a diagonal.
- **Flow has no axis in the cube.** Flow is a cloud / gas / liquid pervading the 3-axis cube of Weight × Space × Time (p. 361). Flow colours but does not locate. Time is the white that emerges from RGB combination — a structural prerequisite, not just a fourth peer.
- **Inner Quests are TOOLS for the actor, not analytical categories.** They are interrogatives that "act as an irritant, provoking the emergence of a latent Mental Factor into an active Inner Participation" (p. 339). Asking "what?" is sensing-irritation; "where?" is thinking-irritation; "when?" is intuiting-irritation; "why?" is feeling-irritation. They mirror the Parsifal/Oedipus mythic structure where the right question redeems.
- **The Mental Factor → Inner Participation transition needs an external catalyst.** The actor cannot self-arouse; the Objective is the activator (p. 282). Receptive emotional-neutrality (martial-arts state) is the prerequisite, not relaxed-on-the-beach. (p. 267)
